answers.intake_q1: When did the elbow pain start?
answers.intake_q2: Can you describe the location of the pain on your elbow and if it radiates anywhere?
answers.intake_q3: Any recent injury or trauma to the elbow?
answers.intake_a3: no, but the pain started while I was playing tennis
answers.intake_q4: Any swelling, redness, or warmth around the elbow?
answers.intake_q5: Any numbness, tingling, or weakness in your arm or hand?
answers.intake_q6: Any difficulty or pain when you try to straighten or bend your elbow?
answers.intake_q7: Any pain or tenderness when you press on the outside of your elbow?
answers.intake_q8: Have you tried any treatments or medications for the elbow pain, and if so, what was the effect?
answers.intake_a8: I iced it the first day and it help a little with the pain
answers.intake_q9: Any pain when gripping or lifting objects, especially with your palm facing down?
answers.intake_q10: Any fever, chills, or other systemic symptoms?

note.soap:
S: 
The patient is a 45-year-old female presenting with a 3-week history of pain localized to the outside of her right elbow. The pain began while playing tennis and is associated with tenderness on palpation of the lateral elbow. She denies any recent trauma, swelling, redness, warmth, numbness, tingling, or weakness. There is no difficulty bending or straightening the elbow, but she experiences pain when gripping or lifting objects, especially with the palm facing down. Initial icing provided some mild relief. She reports no systemic symptoms such as fever or chills. Patient is not pregnant.

O: 
Phone evaluation only; no physical exam or vital signs available.

A: 
The clinical presentation is consistent with lateral epicondylitis (tennis elbow), characterized by lateral elbow pain exacerbated by gripping and wrist extension activities. This is a common overuse injury in tennis players and others performing repetitive wrist extension.

Differential: 
1. Lateral epicondylitis (tennis elbow) 
2. Radial tunnel syndrome 
3. Osteoarthritis of the elbow 
4. Referred pain from cervical radiculopathy 

P: 
- First-line medication: Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) such as ibuprofen or naproxen for pain relief and inflammation reduction. 
- Alternatives: Topical NSAIDs or acetaminophen if NSAIDs are contraindicated or not tolerated. 
- Medication safety considerations: Use NSAIDs with caution in patients with gastrointestinal, renal, or cardiovascular risk factors; avoid in patients with known allergies to NSAIDs. Acetaminophen should be used within recommended dosing limits to avoid hepatotoxicity. 
- Return precautions: Advise the patient to seek care if pain worsens, if there is new swelling, redness, warmth, numbness, weakness, or if systemic symptoms such as fever develop. 
- Testing: Not indicated at this time; consider imaging or referral if symptoms persist beyond 6-12 weeks or if diagnosis is unclear.

evidence.title: Elbow Injuries | Elbow Disorders | MedlinePlus
evidence.snippet: bone, cartilage, ligaments and fluid. Muscles and tendons help the elbow joint move. When any of these structures is hurt or diseased, you have elbow problems. Many things can make your elbow hurt. A common cause is tendinitis , an inflammation or injury to the tendons that attach muscle to bone. Tendinitis of the elbow is a sports injury , often from playin…
